sourceMovie.title: Three
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Three (2005) stuck because it commits to its adventure premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Stewart Raffill's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The adventure and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Cast Away
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Three clicked for you, Cast Away (2000) is a strong next stop: both trade in adventure and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Three clicked for you, The Lighthouse (2019) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and thriller, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Illusionist
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Three clicked for you, The Illusionist (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and thriller, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Three?
faq.answer: Try Cast Away, The Lighthouse, The Illusionist—each shares DNA with Three while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Three?
faq.answer: Three (2005) is best described as Adventure, Drama, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Three worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 5.6/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this adventure lane.
